saudi_arabia_crude: Saudi crude exports partially recovered to ~4.5 mb/d (23–28 Mar) from the 2.8 mb/d trough of 9–15 Mar as the East-West Pipeline ramped to its full 7 mb/d throughput — but remain 29% below the pre-crisis 6.4 mb/d rate, capped by Yanbu port loading bottlenecks (practical capacity ~3–4 mb/d per Vortexa).

Customer mix has fundamentally reshuffled: China (1.3 mb/d) and India (1.2 mb/d) now absorb 55% of Saudi exports, displacing Japan, South Korea, and Egypt — all of whom have dropped out since the crisis began, likely drawing on IEA SPR releases and pivoting to Atlantic Basin barrels.
Myanmar emerged as a new buyer at 335 kbd (23–28 Mar), suggesting Yangon is opportunistically sourcing discounted Yanbu-loaded cargoes as traditional Asian buyers compete for non-Gulf alternatives.

Per Fortune (Mar 28), the pipeline is pumping at 7 mb/d but 70–75% of Yanbu-loaded crude must transit the Bab el-Mandeb on VLCCs to reach Asia, exposing these flows to Houthi interference — a second chokepoint risk that could cap the recovery.
saudi_arabia_lpg: Saudi LPG exports remain at trace levels (~3.8 ktons/d, 23–28 Mar) versus the kingdom's normal ~200+ ktons/d, as offshore NGL production cuts and the Mar 2–13 Ras Tanura shutdown have starved feedstock; small flows to India via Yanbu are the first sign of partial rerouting.

With Qatar's LPG entirely offline and Saudi volumes near zero, the two largest Middle Eastern LPG suppliers have been simultaneously eliminated — per ICIS, Chinese PDH plant operators are scrambling for US Gulf Coast cargoes at sharply higher prices.
saudi_arabia_kero_jet: Saudi jet/kerosene exports collapsed to zero in 23–28 Mar after briefly recovering to 95 kbd in 16–22 Mar (Netherlands cargo), as Yanbu pipeline capacity is being rationed toward crude over refined products — a rational allocation given crude's higher margin at $100+/bbl Brent.

The UK (previously 95 kbd) and Jordan have disappeared entirely as buyers since the crisis began, likely sourcing from US Gulf Coast or European refiners as Red Sea transit risks compound the Hormuz closure.
saudi_arabia_gasoil_diesel: Saudi diesel exports fell 20% to ~593 kbd (23–28 Mar) from 743 kbd pre-crisis, with European buyers (France, Albania, Spain, Italy) completely dropping out — replaced by Egypt, Belgium, Pakistan, and Sudan as the customer base pivots from Mediterranean to Red Sea-accessible markets.

Egypt remains the anchor buyer at ~127 kbd consistently across all four weeks, benefiting from Yanbu-to-Suez proximity; Belgium's emergence at 124 kbd suggests some Yanbu cargoes are successfully reaching NW Europe via the Cape route, though at significantly longer transit times and higher freight costs.
The structural shift from European to African/South Asian customers signals a potential long-term reorientation of Saudi product trade, especially if Hormuz remains closed through Q2.
saudi_arabia_gasoline: Saudi gasoline exports collapsed 54% to ~102 kbd (23–28 Mar) from 224 kbd pre-crisis, as domestic refinery throughput drops with crude production cuts — only Kenya (52 kbd) and Singapore (50 kbd) received cargoes in the latest week.

The customer base has been volatile week-to-week (Oman, Djibouti, Sudan, Kenya rotating through), reflecting spot-driven cargo placement from Yanbu rather than the stable long-term contract flows that characterized pre-crisis Saudi product trade.
saudi_arabia_naphtha: Saudi naphtha exports surged 6x from 13 kbd pre-crisis (2–8 Mar) to 85–120 kbd in subsequent weeks, as Yanbu refinery processing generates excess naphtha that is being placed into Asian petrochemical feedstock markets — the only Saudi product category showing growth during the crisis.

Indonesia has emerged as the primary buyer (85 kbd in 23–28 Mar), displacing pre-crisis buyer Japan — likely reflecting Pertamina's opportunistic procurement of discounted Yanbu-sourced naphtha as Hormuz-dependent UAE naphtha supply dried up for two consecutive weeks.
saudi_arabia_sulphur: Saudi sulphur exports fell to zero in 23–28 Mar (from 10.6 ktons/d pre-crisis), as reduced sour gas processing from offshore field shut-ins has eliminated feedstock — Indonesia and Lithuania have both disappeared as buyers.

Combined with Qatar and Kuwait sulphur shutdowns, the Middle East's near-total sulphur export halt is tightening global fertilizer input markets heading into the Northern Hemisphere spring planting season — the UN's Mar 27 Hormuz humanitarian transit agreement partly addresses this, but volumes will be minimal.
uae_crude: UAE crude exports rebounded sharply to ~3.2 mb/d (23–28 Mar) after cratering to 1.3 mb/d in 16–22 Mar, as ADCOP pipeline throughput to Fujairah increased to 1.62 mb/d (per Kpler) — now at 71% of nameplate capacity with surge potential to 1.8 mb/d.

The customer base has completely rotated: Thailand, Singapore, China, and Malaysia — all Hormuz-dependent — have vanished, replaced by India (933 kbd), South Korea (642 kbd), Japan (317 kbd), and the Philippines (167 kbd), all sourcing via Fujairah's Indian Ocean access.

India's surge from zero to 933 kbd as the top UAE buyer reflects both Fujairah-loaded cargoes and potentially Iran's Mar 26 announcement allowing Indian vessels through Hormuz — though drone attacks on Fujairah port (per ADNOC) remain an ongoing disruption risk to loadings.
uae_naphtha: UAE naphtha exports were offline for two consecutive weeks (9–22 Mar) before resuming at 54 kbd to Taiwan in 23–28 Mar — consistent with the broader Fujairah/ADCOP ramp-up, suggesting Ruwais refinery may be partially restarting despite reported drone damage.

China, the sole pre-crisis buyer at 80 kbd, has been replaced by Taiwan, likely reflecting competitive dynamics as Chinese refiners pivot to Russian and US naphtha while Taiwanese crackers take advantage of available Fujairah-loaded cargoes.
iraq_crude: Iraq's crude exports collapsed 89% from 2.2 mb/d (2–8 Mar) to just 250 kbd (23–28 Mar), all flowing through the restarted Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey — Basra's southern terminals remain entirely shut as storage has filled following the Mar 20 FM declaration on all foreign-operated fields.

China (692 kbd), India (168 kbd), Italy (143 kbd), and Malaysia (143 kbd) have all disappeared as buyers; Turkey is now the sole customer at Kirkuk-Ceyhan's tested maximum throughput of 250 kbd — per The National, another ~100 km of pipeline rehabilitation remains before capacity could be expanded.

The 90% export collapse represents the loss of ~2 mb/d from global supply, compounding the broader Gulf disruption; even if Kirkuk-Ceyhan is pushed to its historical 500 kbd capacity, Iraq would still be exporting at one-quarter of pre-crisis levels.
iraq_naphtha: Iraq's naphtha exports are effectively negligible (sporadic ~15 kbd flows in isolated weeks), consistent with the near-total Basra refinery shutdown that has eliminated downstream product output; any residual volumes likely represent small northern Iraq truck or pipeline-linked flows.
kuwait_crude: Kuwait's crude exports have been effectively zeroed out since 9 Mar following KPC's FM declaration and the devastating Iranian drone strikes on Al-Ahmadi refinery (730 kbd capacity) on Mar 19–20 — the brief 78 kbd blip in 16–22 Mar likely represents in-transit cargoes loaded before the shutdown rather than new loadings.

With no pipeline bypass route (unlike Saudi Arabia, UAE, and Iraq), Kuwait is the most structurally stranded Gulf exporter — full recovery is impossible until Hormuz reopens AND Al-Ahmadi is repaired, a process per Kuwait News Agency that could take 12+ months.
China, the sole identifiable buyer pre-crisis (286 kbd), has entirely disappeared; Kuwait's 1.2 mb/d of pre-crisis exports represent a permanent loss from the market until the conflict resolves.
kuwait_sulphur: Kuwait's sulphur exports are negligible and trending to zero (from 2.5 ktons/d to nothing in 23–28 Mar), consistent with the near-total refinery and processing shutdown following the Al-Ahmadi drone strikes — any residual volumes represent pre-loaded parcels clearing rather than active production.
oman_crude: Oman's crude exports rebounded to ~1.3 mb/d (23–28 Mar) — the highest rate in the four-week window and above the pre-crisis 1.1 mb/d — confirming Oman's unique strategic advantage as the only major Gulf exporter loading entirely outside the Strait of Hormuz at Mina Al-Fahal.

China absorbs nearly 100% of Omani crude (1.27 mb/d), up 14% from pre-crisis levels, as Beijing aggressively secures non-Hormuz Gulf barrels; per Argus Media, Omani grades are commanding significant spot premiums as a result.

The Salalah drone strike on Mar 11 damaged port oil tanks but did not impair crude loading operations at Mina Al-Fahal — Oman's geographic insulation from the Hormuz chokepoint is proving to be an increasingly valuable strategic asset.
oman_lng: Oman LNG exports fell to 24.7 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) from 43 ktons/d early March, despite being one of the few Middle Eastern LNG operations still running — with the 10.4 Mtpa plant near full utilization, the lower loadings likely reflect vessel scheduling gaps rather than production constraints.

India has become the anchor customer (12.9 ktons/d), consistent with Petronet LNG's desperate search for replacement cargoes after Qatar's FM declaration; South Korea and Taiwan have dropped out, likely outbid by Indian and Thai buyers willing to pay the surging +39% Asian spot premium.

Thailand's emergence as a new buyer (5.9 ktons/d) reflects its growing LNG import dependency as domestic gas from the Gulf of Thailand declines — with Qatar offline, Oman is one of very few accessible Asian-proximate LNG suppliers.
oman_kero_jet: Oman's jet/kerosene exports are small and erratic (0–25 kbd), with Mauritius and Djibouti rotating as one-off cargo buyers — the Duqm refinery's limited product slate means these flows are opportunistic spot placements rather than structural trade.
oman_gasoil_diesel: Oman's diesel exports fell 21% to ~114 kbd (23–28 Mar) from 144 kbd pre-crisis, with long-haul buyers (Namibia, Argentina, Madagascar) dropping out — replaced by Pakistan (59 kbd) and Mauritius (55 kbd) as shipping costs and Bab el-Mandeb risks redirect Omani products to the Indian Ocean basin.

Pakistan's emergence as a consistent diesel buyer from Oman (~49–59 kbd since 9 Mar) reflects Islamabad's scramble for fuel as traditional Saudi and Kuwaiti cargoes have disappeared — Oman's non-Hormuz position makes it one of the few reliable Gulf diesel suppliers.
oman_gasoline: Oman's gasoline exports surged 35% to 140 kbd (23–28 Mar) — the highest rate in four weeks — with Saudi Arabia appearing as a buyer at 55 kbd, an unusual intra-Gulf flow suggesting the kingdom is sourcing Omani product to cover domestic shortages as its own refineries run below capacity.

Pakistan remains the top gasoline buyer at 85 kbd (up from 72 kbd pre-crisis), consistent with the broader pattern of Pakistan pivoting hard to Oman as a primary fuel supplier now that Hormuz-dependent sources are cut off.
qatar_crude: Qatar's crude exports are effectively zeroed out — the brief 117 kbd flow in 16–22 Mar went entirely to the UAE (64 kbd daily), likely a short-range barge transfer for Fujairah feedstock rather than a maritime export through Hormuz.

All Qatar offshore fields (~440 kbd including Al Shaheen) remain shut under QatarEnergy's comprehensive FM, with Laffan Refineries 1 and 2 also offline — per Bloomberg, restart depends entirely on Hormuz reopening as Qatar has no pipeline bypass infrastructure whatsoever.
qatar_lng: Qatar LNG shows minimal residual flows (21.5 ktons/d in 23–28 Mar) from 77 Mtpa nameplate — these likely represent stored cargoes or partially loaded vessels clearing Ras Laffan, not new production, given Iranian strikes damaged LNG Trains 4 and 6 (12.8 Mtpa) with repairs estimated at 3–5 years.

Kuwait is the sole recipient, receiving emergency LNG deliveries for domestic power generation via its Al-Zour FSRU — an ironic reversal where one crisis victim supplies another, underscoring the desperate intra-Gulf energy interdependencies exposed by the conflict.

The removal of ~20% of global LNG supply has driven Asian spot prices up 39% and European TTF up 52% per Reuters — with physical damage to production trains, even a ceasefire would not fully restore Qatari LNG for years.
qatar_sulphur: Qatar's sulphur exports have collapsed to zero alongside LNG/crude shutdowns, removing ~8 Mt/year from the global sulphur market — combined with Saudi and Kuwait shutdowns, the Middle East supply gap is critically tightening fertilizer feedstock availability during the Northern Hemisphere spring planting window.
iran_crude: Iran's crude exports declined 28% to ~1.3 mb/d (23–28 Mar) from ~1.85 mb/d pre-crisis, as USAF strikes near Kharg Island (94% of Iran's export capacity) and Israeli strikes on South Pars gas fields have disrupted loading operations — though critically, Kharg's oil infrastructure has been spared deliberate targeting so far.

China, previously the sole identifiable buyer at 658 kbd (2–8 Mar), has disappeared from destination data since 9 Mar — likely reflecting intensified sanctions-evasion measures (STS transfers, AIS spoofing) rather than actual cessation of trade; per Kpler, 68% of Iranian crude still moves on shadow-fleet tankers.

Iran's Mar 26 announcement allowing China, Russia, India, Iraq, and Pakistan to transit Hormuz could theoretically boost loadings, but volumes remain constrained by South Pars gas shut-ins (~12% of output offline per S&P Global), the USAF warning that Kharg could be next, and buyer wariness of escalation risk.
iran_lpg: Iran's LPG exports remain severely curtailed at 6.9 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) versus 9 ktons/d pre-crisis, as the ~30% shut-in of southern NGL-linked oil fields has reduced feedstock — China and India, the sole pre-crisis buyers, have disappeared from destination data, likely rerouted through sanctions-evasion channels rather than halted.

A partial rebound from the 3.2–3.3 ktons/d lows of 9–22 Mar suggests some southern field production is stabilizing, but volumes remain far below pre-conflict levels and recovery is contingent on the broader Hormuz situation.
russia_crude: Russia's crude exports spiked to ~6.8 mb/d (9–15 Mar) as Gulf-dependent buyers scrambled for non-Hormuz barrels, before easing to ~4.8 mb/d (23–28 Mar) — still above the pre-crisis 4.8 mb/d baseline, with China absorbing 52% of export revenue per CREA and ESPO pipeline volumes at record highs.

Customer geography reshaped dramatically: Turkey surged from zero to 519 kbd (16–22 Mar) and France to 455 kbd as European refiners quietly broke from Russian crude avoidance to secure supply; Israel and South Korea disappeared entirely as buyers after mid-March.

India's share contracted sharply from 1.4 mb/d (9–15 Mar) to just 454 kbd (23–28 Mar), consistent with CREA data showing a 19% drop in Feb — Indian refiners may be hitting political limits on visible Russian crude purchases even as supply tightens.

Iran's Mar 26 announcement allowing Chinese, Russian, Indian, Iraqi and Pakistani ships through Hormuz could gradually ease the supply crunch, but Russia remains the primary beneficiary while the strait stays functionally closed to Western-insured tankers.
russia_lng: Russian LNG exports held broadly flat at 81–111 ktons/d through March, with Yamal and Arctic LNG-2 positioned to capture marginal demand from Qatar's 77 Mtpa shutdown — the largest LNG supply deficit in history per ICIS.

Japan emerged as a key new buyer at 21 ktons/d (23–28 Mar), reflecting Tokyo's urgent diversification away from its 93.5% Middle East energy dependence; France and Netherlands dropped out as buyers, likely redirecting to closer US cargoes.

China's purchases swung wildly (8.8→27.7→0 ktons/d) suggesting opportunistic spot buying rather than stable term contracts — consistent with Beijing's strategy of leveraging its Hormuz transit privileges while keeping options open.
russia_lpg: Russian LPG exports remain negligible (<3 ktons/d), limited to sporadic Turkish cargoes — Russia lacks the NGL processing infrastructure to capitalize on the Middle Eastern LPG supply gap despite improving Asian competitiveness.
russia_gasoil_diesel: Russian diesel exports collapsed 65% from 932 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 279 kbd (23–28 Mar), with Turkey — the dominant buyer at 291–411 kbd earlier in March — dropping to just 58 kbd, possibly reflecting storage saturation or tightening sanctions enforcement.

Brazil, Ghana, Sudan, and Senegal all disappeared as buyers by late March, leaving only Turkey, Egypt, and Togo — a dramatic narrowing of Russia's customer base that suggests shadow-fleet logistics are struggling with elevated war-risk insurance and freight rates.

The 70% four-week decline contrasts with Russia's rising crude exports, indicating Moscow is prioritizing raw barrel shipments over refined products — likely running domestic refineries at lower throughput to maximize crude export revenue at $92+/bbl Brent.
russia_naphtha: Russian naphtha exports halved from 632 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 252 kbd (23–28 Mar), with China, India, and South Korea all disappearing as buyers — only Turkey remained at 53 kbd, a dramatic concentration from the six-country customer base pre-crisis.

The loss of Asian petrochem buyers is paradoxical given the Middle Eastern naphtha supply shutdown; elevated freight from Baltic/Black Sea ports plus war-risk premiums may be making Russian naphtha uncompetitive versus closer US Gulf Coast cargoes for Asian crackers.
russia_sulphur: Minimal volumes (~4–13 ktons/d) with China appearing as a new buyer (23–28 Mar) and Brazil dropping off — consistent with the broader pattern of Chinese commodity purchasing shifting toward non-Hormuz suppliers while Gulf sulphur from UAE and Qatar remains offline.
us_crude: US crude exports climbed steadily to 4.3 mb/d (23–28 Mar), up 19% from early March, as the IEA's historic 400 mb coordinated SPR release — 172 mb from the US alone — began flowing from Mar 20, with first deliveries reaching European and Asian buyers.

South Korea surged to become the #2 buyer at 557 kbd (23–28 Mar) from just 116 kbd pre-crisis, and India appeared as a new buyer at 290 kbd — both reflecting urgent diversification away from Gulf crude dependence (Japan's Middle East share is 93.5%, South Korea similarly exposed).

The customer mix shifted decisively toward Asia-Pacific and away from Africa: Nigeria (590 kbd pre-crisis) and Italy (451 kbd) disappeared entirely, replaced by Spain, India, and Brazil — suggesting US barrels are being pulled east by the highest tanker rate premiums since 2005.

Netherlands remained the top buyer at 583 kbd, anchoring Europe's Rotterdam refining hub as the main conduit for US crude into the continent — critical given the EU's refusal to resume Russian crude purchases despite supply tightness.
us_lng: US LNG exports held flat at ~367–371 ktons/d throughout March, confirming that all eight export terminals are running at maximum capacity — the EIA notes Golden Pass Train 1 is coming online this month, which could add marginal supply.

Geographic spread is remarkably wide with 12+ countries receiving cargoes each week, reflecting the ~20% global supply hole left by Qatar's shutdown pulling every available molecule to market; Thailand emerged as a new significant buyer at 40 ktons/d (23–28 Mar).

Despite operating at capacity, US LNG cannot meaningfully offset Qatar's 77 Mtpa loss — per AAF analysis, the supply gap exceeds all available spare capacity globally, keeping Asian JKM spot prices elevated ~39% above pre-crisis levels.
us_lpg: US LPG exports rose 22% to 174 ktons/d (23–28 Mar), with Indonesia surging to the top buyer at 13.4 ktons/d as Middle Eastern NGL-linked supply from Saudi Arabia, UAE, and Qatar remains offline — consistent with PDH plant operators scrambling for US cargoes per ICIS.

Japan's share declined from 34 to 11 ktons/d over four weeks while Mexico held steady at ~12 ktons/d, suggesting Japanese buyers may be drawing down inventories or securing Australian cargoes while Latin American demand remains structurally inelastic.

The LPG-naphtha spread continues to favor US exports per Argus, and the widening of the customer base to include Egypt, Colombia, and France signals that Gulf LPG supply disruption is pulling US volumes into non-traditional markets.
us_kero_jet: US jet fuel exports nearly doubled from 154 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 265 kbd (23–28 Mar), with the UK appearing as a major new buyer at 102 kbd — consistent with Europe facing jet fuel records above $90/bbl as 30% of the continent's supply normally transits Hormuz.

Netherlands also emerged at 50 kbd, routing US jet fuel through Rotterdam for European distribution; the traditional Caribbean/Latin American customer base (Panama, Costa Rica) persisted alongside these new transatlantic flows.

The +48% four-week surge makes jet/kero the fastest-growing US export category, reflecting that middle distillates are the tightest refined product globally with diesel cracks exceeding $50–70/bbl at peaks per market data.
us_gasoil_diesel: US diesel exports surged 52% to 1.4 mb/d (23–28 Mar), with Netherlands exploding from 10 kbd pre-crisis to 467 kbd — now the #1 buyer, channeling US diesel into Europe's Rotterdam hub as EU refiners face their worst middle-distillate squeeze since the 2022 Russia embargo.

UK purchases doubled to 151 kbd while Morocco appeared as a new buyer at 51 kbd, both reflecting Europe and North Africa drawing emergency diesel volumes across the Atlantic as Gulf product flows remain near zero.

Latin American demand remains robust with Mexico (71 kbd), Peru (51 kbd), and Chile (51 kbd) maintaining steady purchases, but the dramatic reweighting toward Europe suggests transatlantic arbitrage economics now dominate the US export barrel.
us_gasoline: US gasoline exports rose 31% to 923 kbd (23–28 Mar), with Mexico remaining the anchor buyer at 178 kbd though its share has declined as new markets open — Australia appeared for the first time at 51 kbd, a buyer normally served by Asian refineries now disrupted.

Colombia surged to 71 kbd and the Dominican Republic to 50 kbd, while Australia's appearance signals that the Gulf crisis is redirecting refined product trade flows globally, with Asia-Pacific buyers previously served by Middle Eastern and Indian refineries now pulling from the US Gulf Coast.
us_naphtha: US naphtha exports surged to 738 kbd (16–22 Mar) — more than double the early-March rate — as South Korea appeared with a massive 203 kbd draw, compensating for lost Middle Eastern and Russian naphtha supply to its petrochemical crackers.

Japan also ramped to 126 kbd (16–22 Mar), reflecting Asia's urgent pivot to non-Gulf naphtha as both Russian and Middle Eastern supplies contracted; the subsequent W13 pullback to 439 kbd suggests the initial restocking surge is moderating.

Venezuela remained a steady buyer at 73–84 kbd, likely for its refinery blending operations, while Colombia emerged as a new destination — diversifying the buyer base beyond the traditional Western Hemisphere naphtha trade.
us_sulphur: US sulphur exports are negligible and sporadic (0–6 ktons/d), with only occasional cargoes to Mexico and Belgium — the US is not a significant sulphur exporter and unlikely to fill the gap from offline Gulf producers (UAE, Qatar).
australia_crude: Australia's crude exports declined to 213 kbd (23–28 Mar) from 267 kbd pre-crisis, with South Korea emerging as the sole buyer at 124 kbd — a dramatic concentration from the three-country base (Indonesia, Singapore, Thailand) that existed in early March.

Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand all disappeared as buyers despite geographic proximity, likely because these countries are securing alternative Gulf-linked supply via Iran's partial Hormuz reopening to select Asian nations — Australia's condensate-heavy crude may be less suitable for their refinery configurations.

South Korea's dominance reflects emergency sourcing: Korean refiners face acute Middle East supply disruption and are paying premium freight to secure every available non-Hormuz barrel, even from distant Australia.
australia_lng: Australian LNG exports fell 21% to 157 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) despite Qatar's 77 Mtpa global shutdown creating unprecedented demand — confirming per AAF and EIA analysis that Australia's liquefaction plants face seasonal and contractual constraints that prevent surge production.

Japan remained the anchor buyer at 70 ktons/d, while India appeared for the first time at 9.3 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) — reflecting Petronet LNG's scramble for replacement cargoes after declaring FM on Qatari supplies; Thailand also grew steadily from 11 to 23 ktons/d.

Taiwan disappeared entirely after receiving 53 ktons/d in 9–15 Mar, and China contracted from 58 to 24 ktons/d — both may be diverting procurement toward Russian LNG (leveraging Hormuz transit privileges) or drawing on strategic reserves.

The inability to ramp Australian output means Asia's LNG deficit persists: with Qatar offline, US at capacity, and Australia constrained, spot JKM prices remain 39%+ above pre-crisis levels with no near-term relief.
australia_lpg: Australian LPG exports are negligible (0–6 ktons/d), with China as the only meaningful buyer in sporadic cargoes — Australia lacks the NGL processing capacity to be a significant LPG exporter.
australia_naphtha: Sporadic exports (~49–58 kbd in alternate weeks) to Singapore and Malaysia only — Australia is not a structural naphtha exporter, and these small volumes likely reflect condensate splitter byproduct rather than strategic supply.
eu_27_crude: EU crude re-exports remain small and volatile at 46–435 kbd daily, reflecting intra-European storage optimization rather than strategic trade — Brazil and Turkey dropped off while Netherlands appeared as a new re-export route.

The -67% four-week decline to 168 kbd (23–28 Mar) likely reflects EU refineries maximizing crude processing runs to capture record diesel and jet fuel crack spreads ($50–70/bbl), leaving less crude available for re-export.
eu_27_lpg: EU LPG exports are minimal at 2–5 ktons/d, dominated by Poland (~1.5 ktons/d for intra-regional redistribution) — the EU is a net LPG importer and these small re-export volumes are not market-relevant.
eu_27_kero_jet: EU jet fuel exports collapsed 86% from 69 kbd (2–8 Mar) to just 12 kbd (23–28 Mar), with only UK receiving the residual — consistent with Europe facing acute jet fuel shortages as 30% of supply normally transits Hormuz and spot premiums hit records above $90/bbl.

Denmark disappeared as a buyer (consistent with Danish FM declaration), and the mid-March spike to 146 kbd (9–15 Mar) when France took 51 kbd appears to have been a one-off inventory transfer before the continent pivoted to importing US jet fuel instead of re-exporting.
eu_27_gasoil_diesel: EU diesel exports fell 43% from 1.1 mb/d (9–15 Mar peak) to 430 kbd (23–28 Mar) as the bloc retained more product domestically amid the worst distillate squeeze since the 2022 Russia ban — diesel crack spreads more than doubled to $70+/bbl per market data.

UK was the top buyer through mid-March at 204 kbd (16–22 Mar) but Croatia surged to 91 kbd in late March, reflecting intra-European redistribution to supply-constrained Adriatic refineries; Poland remained a steady 58–245 kbd buyer throughout.
Germany and Romania disappeared as buyers by late March, consistent with both countries' FM-linked refinery run cuts and domestic retention of available diesel supply.
eu_27_gasoline: EU gasoline exports held steady at ~1.1–1.2 mb/d throughout March despite the broader energy crisis — the only EU product category to maintain or grow export volumes, reflecting Europe's structural gasoline surplus from diesel-optimized refineries.

Destination mix shifted dramatically toward West Africa: Ghana (99 kbd), Kenya (111 kbd), Senegal (58 kbd), and Mauritania (53 kbd) appeared as major buyers in the second half of March, replacing Libya and Netherlands which dominated early March — suggesting Gulf-sourced African gasoline supply has been disrupted.

The appearance of Saudi Arabia as a 75 kbd buyer (9–15 Mar) is notable — the kingdom is typically a gasoline exporter, and this import signals domestic refinery disruptions from Ras Tanura shutdown and offshore field closures constraining Saudi product supply.
eu_27_naphtha: EU naphtha exports remain volatile at 235–423 kbd, with China appearing as a new buyer at 73 kbd (9–15 Mar) and 58 kbd (23–28 Mar) — Chinese petrochemical plants are sourcing naphtha globally as Gulf and Russian supplies contract.

Japan drew 50 kbd (16–22 Mar), reflecting Asian crackers' urgent diversification; the -32% four-week decline suggests EU refineries are also retaining more naphtha as feedstock amid elevated crude costs and margins.
china_crude: China's crude imports collapsed 42% from ~14.5 mb/d (2–8 Mar) to ~8.4 mb/d (23–28 Mar) as the Hormuz closure severed access to Gulf barrels — Gulf share fell from 44% to 29%, the lowest in modern tracking.

Russia remains the anchor supplier at ~2.4 mb/d, but the standout shift is Oman surging to 1.8 mb/d (23–28 Mar) from 1.1 mb/d pre-crisis, exploiting its non-Hormuz export position at Mina Al-Fahal to capture displaced Gulf demand.

Saudi Arabia and Iraq have effectively disappeared from China's import slate: Iraq's FM declaration on all foreign-operated fields (Basra output collapsed to ~800 kb/d per Reuters) and Saudi's Hormuz-dependent eastern terminals explain the loss, despite Yanbu pipeline rerouting prioritising other customers.

Pipeline imports (ESPO ~600, Kazakhstan ~220, Myanmar ~200 kbd) provide ~1.0 mb/d of insulated baseload, but cover only 12% of pre-crisis seaborne volumes — Beijing's Jan–Feb stockpiling at ~12.5 mb/d (Bloomberg) is the real buffer being drawn on now.
china_lng: Qatar vanished entirely from China's LNG imports by 23–28 Mar (0 ktons/d, down from 51.8 ktons/d in early March), following QatarEnergy's FM extension to Chinese contracts on Mar 24 — removing ~48% of China's Gulf-sourced LNG in a single declaration.

Australia has become the dominant supplier at 43.4 ktons/d (54% share), with Malaysia, Indonesia, and Russia filling the balance; total imports still fell 26% to 80.8 ktons/d as the global LNG supply gap from Qatar's 77 Mtpa shutdown (~20% of world supply) constrains spot availability.

Wanhua Chemical's FM declaration on Middle East supplies signals downstream industrial disruption; Power of Siberia pipeline gas provides partial insulation but cannot replace seaborne LNG volumes at current capacity.
china_lpg: China's LPG imports held relatively steady at ~76 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) despite Qatar and Kuwait dropping out entirely, as US Gulf Coast cargoes surged to 39.2 ktons/d (51% share) — the highest US concentration in the dataset.

Iran's LPG volumes fluctuated (12.3 → 21.2 → 17.8 ktons/d), likely reflecting sanctions-constrained ship-to-ship transfers being redirected as Gulf alternatives vanished; PDH plant operators are scrambling for non-Gulf supply at sharply higher prices per ICIS.

The complete loss of Saudi, Qatari, and Kuwaiti NGL-linked LPG is structural for the duration of the crisis — China's position as the world's largest LPG importer makes it acutely exposed to the LPG-naphtha spread shift as both feedstocks face Gulf supply constraints.
china_gasoil_diesel: China's gasoil/diesel imports collapsed 86% from ~99 kbd (2–8 Mar) to ~14 kbd (23–28 Mar), with Japan and Chile disappearing entirely as suppliers — South Korea is now the sole source at 13.7 kbd.

The sharp drawdown reflects both tightening regional product availability (as Asian refiners cut crude runs due to Gulf supply disruption) and China's substantial domestic refining capacity acting as a buffer; this flow is supplementary rather than critical.
china_naphtha: China's naphtha supply chain was completely restructured in four weeks: Gulf share crashed from 77% (2–8 Mar) to 0% (23–28 Mar) as Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE, and India all disappeared from the supplier slate.

Russia emerged as the top replacement at 124 kbd (38% share, 23–28 Mar), joined by Norway (89 kbd), South Korea (58 kbd), and Thailand (55 kbd) — an entirely new supply geography assembled in under a month.

Despite the supplier overhaul, total naphtha imports only fell 19% (403 → 326 kbd), suggesting China's petrochemical buyers moved faster than peers to secure alternative barrels — but the naphtha price surge from $622 to $756/mt (C&F Japan, per Platts) is compressing margins regardless.
china_sulphur: China's sulphur imports fell 60% to 5.4 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) with only Canada supplying, after Saudi Arabia disappeared and UAE/Qatar/Kuwait volumes dried up — sulphur is a byproduct of sour crude/gas processing, so production is directly tied to the upstream shut-ins across the Gulf.
The supply loss is structural: with Gulf refining and gas processing at reduced capacity indefinitely, China's fertiliser and chemicals sectors face sustained sulphur tightness.
india_crude: India's crude imports recovered to ~5.1 mb/d (23–28 Mar) after dipping to 3.6 mb/d in mid-March, driven by a massive Russia pivot: Russian barrels hit 1.9 mb/d (38% share), with Bloomberg tracking at least seven tankers rerouting from China to India after New Delhi secured a US sanctions waiver.

Iraq has effectively collapsed as a supplier (from 653 kbd to 71 kbd) following the Mar 20 FM declaration on all foreign-operated fields — Basra's output at ~800 kb/d (from 3.3 mb/d) cannot sustain export volumes.

The supplier mix has fundamentally reshuffled: UAE (567 kbd) and Nigeria (481 kbd) appeared as new top-5 sources, while Saudi recovered to 1.1 mb/d via Yanbu pipeline barrels — but MRPL's FM declaration on gasoline exports signals refinery margins remain under acute pressure from $102/bbl Brent.

Gulf share rebounded to 35% from a crisis low of 6% (9–15 Mar), but the pre-crisis 44% dependence is unlikely to return while Hormuz remains closed; Russia's structural entrenchment at 38–53% of India's slate is the new baseline.
india_lng: India's LNG imports surged to 81.1 ktons/d (23–28 Mar), recovering from a low of 31.2 ktons/d, as Oman's non-Hormuz LNG facility (10.4 Mtpa, one of the few still operating in the Middle East) resumed dominant supply at 37.5 ktons/d.

The US appeared as India's second-largest LNG source at 22.5 ktons/d, plus Nigerian (10 ktons/d) and Angolan (11.2 ktons/d) cargoes — a rapid diversification from the pre-crisis position where Qatar supplied 41% of India's LNG (11.2 MT/yr) before Petronet LNG declared FM.

Gulf share swung violently (82% → 0% → 46%) as Oman's cargoes came and went — India remains structurally exposed until alternative US/Australian long-term contracts can be secured to replace the lost Qatari volumes.
india_lpg: India's LPG imports fell 56% to 13.3 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) as UAE, Bahrain, and Saudi Arabia all disappeared from the supplier slate — a direct threat to the Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala cooking fuel programme serving 100M+ households.

The US has become India's primary LPG source at 8.7 ktons/d (65% share), with only residual Qatari volumes (4.7 ktons/d) surviving; Gulf share collapsed from 75% pre-crisis to 35%, and replacement US Gulf Coast cargoes come at a significant cost premium per Economic Times reporting.
india_gasoil_diesel: India's gasoil/diesel imports flipped entirely from UAE (8.5 kbd pre-crisis) to Russia (18.3 kbd, 23–28 Mar) after a complete supply halt in 9–15 Mar — a microcosm of the broader Russia pivot across India's energy imports.
Volumes remain small (~18 kbd) given India's large domestic refining base, but the 100% Russian sourcing underscores how deeply the Hormuz closure has reoriented even marginal product flows.
india_gasoline: India's gasoline imports are minimal (~7.5 kbd, all from South Korea) and volatile, reflecting India's self-sufficiency in refining — but MRPL's FM declaration on gasoline exports and 500 kb/d refinery run cuts suggest domestic supply tightness could drive increased import needs if the crisis persists.
india_naphtha: India's naphtha import geography was completely rewritten: Gulf share fell from 100% (2–8 Mar, all UAE/Kuwait/Iran) to 0% (16–22 Mar, Russia only) before partially recovering to 52% via Oman (54 kbd) in the latest week.

Russia has established itself as a structural naphtha supplier at ~51 kbd (23–28 Mar), a flow that didn't exist pre-crisis; combined with Oman's non-Hormuz position, India's petrochemical feedstock chain has found interim alternatives but at volumes 31% below pre-crisis levels.
india_sulphur: India's sulphur imports are highly sporadic (0 → 4.7 → 0 → 12.3 ktons/d across four weeks), with Qatar's single mid-March cargo replaced by Canadian (10.2 ktons/d) and Jordanian (2.1 ktons/d) volumes in the latest week — a rare commodity where non-Gulf alternatives exist but at irregular intervals.
japan_crude: Japan's crude imports crashed 54% to ~960 kbd (23–28 Mar) from ~2.1 mb/d sustained through mid-March, confirming Japan's status as the most Hormuz-exposed major importer at 93.5% Middle East dependence (per METI).

The supplier slate remains almost entirely Gulf-dependent (94% share in 23–28 Mar) with no Russia pivot — UAE overtook Saudi as the top supplier (461 vs 404 kbd), likely reflecting ADCOP pipeline-routed Fujairah cargoes, while Kuwait disappeared entirely after the Al-Ahmadi refinery drone strikes.

Japan's 80 mb SPR release (54 mb crude + 26 mb products, part of the IEA's record 400 mb coordinated release) is the primary buffer, but at current ~960 kbd import rates the drawdown is unsustainable for more than a few months without Hormuz reopening or a fundamental supply diversification.
japan_lng: Japan's LNG imports declined 25% to 140 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) with Gulf share falling to 0% in the latest week — Australia dominates at 86 ktons/d (61% share), with Malaysia, Papua New Guinea, and small Russian volumes filling the balance.

Qatar was never a major direct LNG supplier to Japan in the tracked period (peak 10.7 ktons/d), reflecting Japan's pre-crisis diversification to Australian and Southeast Asian contracts; the real risk is price — JKM futures surged from $10.73 to $22.35/MMBtu since the crisis began.

Nuclear restart progress provides some demand-side relief, but Japan's structural LNG dependence for power generation means any prolonged disruption to global supply (with Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline) keeps procurement costs elevated through the summer.
japan_lpg: Japan has completed a full pivot to US LPG: imports averaged 34.7 ktons/d (23–28 Mar), 100% from the United States, up 58% from early March — Australia's small volumes disappeared entirely.

Zero Gulf LPG across all four weeks indicates Japan proactively exited Middle East NGL supply chains before the crisis peak, though this US-only dependency creates concentration risk and exposes Japan to Gulf Coast pricing/logistics constraints.
japan_kero_jet: China disappeared as a jet fuel supplier to Japan after early March (45.1 → 0 kbd), consistent with China's MRPL FM declaration and refinery run cuts curtailing export availability.

South Korea is now Japan's sole jet fuel/kerosene source at 81.7 kbd (23–28 Mar), a critical single-supplier dependency for Japan's aviation sector — any disruption to South Korean refining (which itself faces Gulf crude supply challenges) would cascade immediately.
japan_gasoil_diesel: Japan's gasoil/diesel imports are sporadic and small (0–53 kbd range), sourced opportunistically from South Korea and Brunei; the latest week showed zero imports, suggesting Japan is drawing on domestic stocks and SPR product releases (26 mb allocated) rather than securing regular seaborne diesel.
japan_gasoline: Japan's gasoline imports from South Korea surged 607% to 99.7 kbd (23–28 Mar), a dramatic acceleration that signals Japan is pivoting from crude refining to finished product imports as Gulf crude supply dwindles.

This structural shift — buying South Korean refined products rather than importing crude to process domestically — reflects the arithmetic of 93% Gulf crude dependence meeting a closed Strait: it is cheaper and faster to import products from operational Korean refineries than to source alternative crudes.
japan_naphtha: Japan's naphtha imports experienced wild supplier swings but stayed in the 86–289 kbd range, with Gulf share fluctuating from 100% (early March, UAE/Qatar/Kuwait) to 41% to 100% (Qatar only in 16–22 Mar) to 71% (UAE-led, 23–28 Mar).

Qatar and Kuwait disappeared as regular naphtha suppliers, replaced by Peru (21.8 kbd), South Korea (27.5 kbd), and Algeria (15.1 kbd) — Japanese petrochemical producers are being forced to curb output as naphtha inventories deplete rapidly, per OilPrice.com, with only weeks of stock cover remaining.

UAE naphtha (155.9 kbd in 23–28 Mar, likely Fujairah/ADCOP-routed) is the critical lifeline keeping Japan's petrochemical crackers running, but Fujairah's own vulnerability to drone attacks makes this a fragile supply line.
south_korea_crude: South Korea's crude imports plunged 55% to ~1.5 mb/d (23–28 Mar) from 3.4 mb/d pre-crisis, threatening the country's massive refining complex (SK Energy, S-Oil, GS Caltex, Hyundai — combined ~3.5 mb/d capacity).

UAE surged to the top supplier at 687 kbd (45% share in 23–28 Mar), likely via ADCOP pipeline cargoes from Fujairah, while Saudi fell to 255 kbd and Kuwait disappeared entirely after the Al-Ahmadi drone strikes — Algeria (180 kbd) appeared as a new non-Gulf source.

The SPR release of 22.5 mb (5 mb drawn by Mar 25 per Yonhap) provides weeks not months of cover at the current import deficit; YNCC's FM declaration on petrochemical products signals the supply crunch is already cascading into downstream manufacturing.
south_korea_lng: South Korea's LNG imports fell to 106.2 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) with Gulf share at 0%, as Qatar and Peru disappeared from the supplier slate — but KOGAS's diversified long-term portfolio (80% of 46.7 MT under term deals) is providing resilience.

Australia dominates at 55.8 ktons/d (53%), supplemented by Indonesia (19.9), Malaysia (11.3), and Chinese re-exports (9.7 ktons/d) — the new KOGAS-Trafigura 10-year deal for 3.3 mtpa of mostly US LNG (per S&P Global) signals a permanent diversification away from Gulf LNG dependency.

Seoul's official assessment of 'no LNG shortages' appears justified by the data, but spot procurement costs have doubled (JKM from $10.73 to $22.35/MMBtu), adding billions to annual import bills with direct implications for power generation costs and semiconductor fab energy expenses.
south_korea_lpg: South Korea's LPG imports are already fully de-Gulfed: 100% sourced from the US (7.9 ktons/d, 23–28 Mar), down 47% from early March as Canadian volumes also dropped out, reflecting tightening North American export availability.
The LPG-naphtha price spread has shifted as both feedstocks face Gulf-linked supply constraints per ICIS, squeezing petrochemical crackers that rely on LPG as a naphtha alternative.
south_korea_gasoil_diesel: South Korea's gasoil/diesel imports are minimal and shifting: Japan was the primary source through mid-March, but Russia (8.8 kbd) and China (3.3 kbd) appeared in the latest week (23–28 Mar) — reflecting regional product flow realignment as Gulf-sourced crude runs fall across Asian refiners.
south_korea_gasoline: South Korea's gasoline imports collapsed to zero (23–28 Mar) after peaking at 108 kbd from Japan (9–15 Mar), with only a brief Singapore substitution in between — this likely reflects Korea's own refineries prioritising domestic gasoline production as crude supply tightens.
The volatility (76 → 108 → 24 → 0 kbd) underscores how rapidly refined product trade is being disrupted as crude availability constrains refinery utilisation across the region.
south_korea_naphtha: South Korea's naphtha imports fell 40% to 401 kbd (23–28 Mar) with a dramatic geographic overhaul: UAE, Bahrain, and India disappeared, replaced by Greece (72.7 kbd), Peru (57.6 kbd), Egypt (55 kbd), and Libya (31.1 kbd) — Mediterranean and Latin American barrels never previously seen in Korea's naphtha slate.

Gulf share crashed from 96% (9–15 Mar) to 30% (23–28 Mar), with Oman (81.4 kbd) and Qatar residuals (39.5 kbd) the only remaining Gulf sources; Korean petrochemical producers are structurally operating with only weeks of naphtha stock cover per Alkagesta, and further supply erosion risks forced production curtailment.

The appearance of Greek and Peruvian naphtha in Korea's import mix signals the global naphtha market is being arbitraged to its limits — marginal barrels are being pulled from wherever they exist, at C&F Asia prices up 22% ($756/mt) from pre-crisis levels.
thailand_crude: Thailand's crude imports narrowed to just two suppliers in 23–28 Mar — UAE at ~0.82 mb/d (93% share) and Malaysia (60 kbd) — down from eight sources in early March, as Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, US, and Saudi-Kuwaiti Neutral Zone all dropped out following Hormuz closure and regional FM declarations.

Despite the collapse in supplier diversity, Thailand holds 90+ days of strategic reserves and PTT has been mandated to diversify toward US, West African, and Malaysian barrels — but the current rate of ~0.88 mb/d is trending lower and 10% below the month’s average.

UAE Murban crude via ADCOP pipeline has become Thailand’s lifeline, but ADCOP throughput is already at a record 1.62 mb/d serving multiple Asian buyers — any further ADCOP constraint would leave Thailand critically undersupplied.
thailand_lng: Qatar’s FM removed 52% of Thailand’s LNG supply overnight — imports plunged from 52.4 ktons/d (2–8 Mar) to 25.2 ktons/d (9–15 Mar) before partially recovering to 35.5 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) through emergency spot cargoes from Australia, Oman, Mozambique, and Canada.

Thailand’s Energy Regulatory Commission approved three emergency spot LNG shipments for March–April delivery; Oman emerged as a new non-Hormuz Gulf supplier (11.7 ktons/d), leveraging its strategic export position with Qatar’s 77 Mtpa offline.

With declining domestic gas from the Gulf of Thailand and Myanmar pipeline, Thailand’s growing LNG import dependency is being stress-tested — spot prices above $20/MMBtu per Wood Mackenzie make these replacement cargoes significantly more expensive than pre-crisis contracts.
thailand_lpg: Thailand’s LPG imports remain near zero throughout March (peak of just 0.9 ktons/d from Malaysia in 16–22 Mar), reflecting domestic self-sufficiency from PTT’s gas separation plants and limited exposure to the Gulf LPG supply disruption.
thailand_gasoil_diesel: Thailand’s diesel imports are small but saw a notable spike to 45.5 kbd in 16–22 Mar from India — likely an opportunistic cargo as Indian refiners redirected product from constrained export markets before reverting to Malaysia (9.4 kbd) in the latest week.

China disappeared as diesel supplier after the first week of March, replaced by Malaysia; volumes remain marginal relative to domestic refining capacity but the supplier churn signals regional product market tightness.
thailand_gasoline: Gasoline imports from Singapore — Thailand’s sole supplier — dropped to zero in 23–28 Mar after steady flows of 5–9 kbd earlier in the month, suggesting Singapore’s refiners are retaining product for domestic needs or redirecting to higher-priced markets amid the regional supply squeeze.
thailand_naphtha: Thailand’s naphtha imports collapsed 97% from 113.6 kbd (2–8 Mar) to just 3.7 kbd (23–28 Mar) after Bahrain’s BAPCO Sitra refinery — which supplied 63% of feedstock at 71.8 kbd — was destroyed by missile strikes on Mar 4 and Mar 9.

The loss of Bahraini naphtha is a direct hit to Thai petrochemical crackers; a small Singapore cargo (3.7 kbd) in the latest week cannot replace the ~72 kbd BAPCO was providing, signaling sustained petchem feedstock stress ahead.
vietnam_crude: Vietnam’s crude imports fell to ZERO in 23–28 Mar — the most extreme supply disruption in this batch — after Kuwait, which supplied 100% of imports for Nghi Son Refinery at ~0.29 mb/d, declared FM and Hormuz closed; a single Nigerian cargo (~0.09 mb/d, 16–22 Mar) was the only non-Kuwaiti crude Vietnam received all month.

The government cut MFN oil import tariffs to 0% via Decree 72/2026 (effective Mar 9) and mobilized 4 million barrels from international partners covering 30–45 days, per VietnamNet — but structurally replacing Kuwait’s ~0.29 mb/d of medium-sour feedstock for Nghi Son requires finding compatible crude from entirely new suppliers.

Vietnam’s near-total dependence on a single Gulf supplier is the starkest single-point-of-failure in Asian crude markets — even with emergency measures, Nghi Son will face weeks of sub-capacity operation unless alternative crude arrives imminently.
vietnam_lpg: US Gulf Coast LPG emerged as Vietnam’s primary supplier, surging from zero to 11.9 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) as Chinese LPG dried up — a 2,267% four-week increase driven by Enterprise and Targa terminal capacity filling the ME NGL supply gap.

The shift from Chinese to American LPG reflects both China’s own tightening supply (Wanhua Chemical FM) and the favorable LPG-naphtha spread driving increased US Gulf Coast exports across Asia per Argus LPG World.
vietnam_kero_jet: Jet fuel imports fell 60% to 15.5 kbd (23–28 Mar) from 33.2 kbd pre-crisis, with Thailand and China both disappearing as suppliers — Singapore is now the sole source, reflecting constrained regional availability as Asian refiners cut crude runs.

The loss of Thai jet fuel is directly linked to Thailand’s own export suspension; China’s FM-related crude shortages are curtailing its product exports across the board, leaving Vietnam dependent on a single jet fuel supplier.
vietnam_gasoil_diesel: Vietnam’s diesel imports nearly doubled to 167.9 kbd (23–28 Mar) from 96.1 kbd pre-crisis, dominated by South Korea at 153.4 kbd (91% share) — a direct consequence of Nghi Son Refinery running out of Kuwaiti crude feedstock and domestic refining output collapsing.

South Korea’s naphtha export ban (Mar 27) hasn’t constrained diesel flows to Vietnam yet, but YNCC’s FM on petrochemical products signals Korea’s own supply chain is under stress — Vietnam’s reliance on a single diesel supplier mirrors the Kuwait crude vulnerability that caused this crisis.
vietnam_gasoline: Gasoline imports plunged 81% to 13.7 kbd (23–28 Mar) as Malaysia disappeared and Singapore became the sole supplier at sharply reduced volumes — a downstream consequence of the crude feedstock crisis throttling Nghi Son and Binh Son refinery output.

Vietnam triggered its national fuel contingency plan per state media; domestic refineries are boosting output where possible, but without crude supply restoration, gasoline shortages will intensify as the 4-million-barrel emergency stockpile depletes.
vietnam_sulphur: Vietnam’s sulphur imports — sourced entirely from Qatar for fertilizer production — collapsed to zero following QatarEnergy’s FM; a single Jordanian cargo (2.7 ktons/d, 16–22 Mar) provided temporary relief, but sustained disruption threatens agricultural input supply chains ahead of the planting season.
united_states_crude: US crude imports dropped 29% to ~1.97 mb/d (23–28 Mar) from the ~3.13 mb/d peak (9–15 Mar), softened by the record 172 mb SPR exchange release over 120 days — though per DOE only ~12.5 mb had been delivered through Mar 28, the commitment alone has tempered market panic.

Iraq’s FM cut US-bound shipments from 368 kbd to just 47 kbd as Basra exports collapsed; Saudi Arabia remains the top Gulf supplier at ~499 kbd but Venezuela (387 kbd) and Mexico (259 kbd) now collectively provide more, reflecting an accelerating Western Hemisphere pivot.

The US is largely insulated as the world’s top producer (~13.2 mb/d domestic) with low Gulf dependency (~28% of imports), but with Brent at $112.57/bbl, domestic gasoline and jet fuel costs are rising regardless of import source — the crisis is a price shock more than a supply shock for American consumers.
united_states_lpg: US LPG imports remain near zero — consistent with the US position as the world’s largest LPG exporter; the Gulf crisis is a net tailwind for US Gulf Coast terminals now commanding premium prices to fill the ME NGL supply gap across Asia per Argus LPG World.
united_states_kero_jet: US jet fuel imports surged 469% to 86.4 kbd (23–28 Mar) as South Korea (67.2 kbd) and Taiwan (19.2 kbd) emerged as new suppliers — Asian refiners are redirecting jet/kero cargoes to the US where crack spreads are elevated, a remarkable reversal of typical East→West product flows.

Canada, previously the sole jet fuel supplier at 13 kbd (2–8 Mar), was eclipsed by Korean and Taiwanese refiners exploiting the trans-Pacific arbitrage window; this flow could reverse quickly if Asian domestic demand tightens or export restrictions expand.
united_states_gasoil_diesel: US diesel imports fell 58% to 88.8 kbd (23–28 Mar) from 242 kbd peak (9–15 Mar), with Canada dominant at 79.5 kbd (90% share); Turkey disappeared as a supplier after mid-March, likely retaining product domestically as Mediterranean refining margins tighten.

Argentina appeared as a minor new diesel source (9.3 kbd) — consistent with the broader Latin American supply chain realignment benefiting Southern Cone exporters as traditional trade routes are disrupted.
united_states_gasoline: US gasoline imports dropped 47% to 209 kbd (23–28 Mar) as European suppliers (Netherlands, Belgium) vanished — European refiners are retaining product domestically as their own crude supply from Gulf sources shrinks and the EU’s Russian LNG purchase ban (effective Apr 25) adds further energy anxiety.

The supplier base shifted to Western Hemisphere sources: Canada (127.5 kbd), USVI (48.7 kbd), and Bahamas (18.7 kbd); India provided a one-off 49.3 kbd cargo in 16–22 Mar before MRPL’s FM on gasoline exports shut the door on further Indian flows.
united_states_naphtha: US naphtha imports underwent a complete geographic reset: France, Netherlands, Belgium, and Peru (120.5 kbd combined in 2–8 Mar) all disappeared, replaced by Angola (57.6 kbd), Argentina (17.6 kbd), and Brazil (16.8 kbd) in 23–28 Mar.

European naphtha suppliers are retaining feedstock as their own petrochemical crackers face tightening supply amid reduced Gulf crude throughput; the shift to African and South American sources reflects both necessity and the premium US petchem sector can pay to attract alternative barrels.
taiwan_crude: Taiwan executed the most dramatic supply chain pivot in this batch — from 99.3% Gulf crude (2–8 Mar, Saudi/UAE at ~0.42 mb/d) to 100% US crude (23–28 Mar, ~0.34 mb/d) in just four weeks, as Saudi Arabia, UAE, and Kuwait all dropped out following Hormuz closure.

Daily imports fell 18% to ~0.34 mb/d in 23–28 Mar — well below CPC Corp’s refinery run requirements; Taiwan’s 3-month strategic reserve and contingency planning (restarting idle coal plants per AmCham Taiwan) provide a buffer, but the trajectory is unsustainable.

The US-only crude sourcing is itself a strategic vulnerability — any disruption to trans-Pacific tanker routes or US export constraints would leave Taiwan with zero alternatives, making diversification to West African or Latin American grades an urgent priority.
taiwan_lng: Taiwan’s LNG imports defied the crisis — rising 67% to 83.6 ktons/d (23–28 Mar) from 50.1 ktons/d pre-crisis — through aggressive diversification: Australia surged to 58% of supply (48.3 ktons/d), while Equatorial Guinea (12.1 ktons/d) appeared as an unconventional new Atlantic Basin source alongside Oman and Brunei.

Qatar’s departure after 9–15 Mar (was 26.1 ktons/d, 38% share) forced Taiwan to bid for cargoes globally; per Euronews, Asian spot premiums have pulled LNG eastward from Europe, but Taiwan’s willingness to pay is sustaining supply at significantly higher procurement costs.

Oman (11.7 ktons/d) provides the only remaining Gulf LNG channel, leveraging its non-Hormuz export position as Qatar’s 77 Mtpa remains fully offline — Oman LNG is commanding significant Asian spot premiums per Platts.
taiwan_gasoil_diesel: Taiwan began importing diesel for the first time in March — going from zero to ~35 kbd (23–28 Mar) sourced from South Korea (21.4 kbd) and China (13.7 kbd), signaling that CPC Corp’s domestic refining capacity is constrained by the crude supply disruption.

This is a structural shift: Taiwan was self-sufficient in diesel pre-crisis, and the new import dependence on South Korea and China creates exposure to both countries’ own supply chain stresses, including South Korea’s expanding export restrictions.
taiwan_gasoline: Taiwan imported a one-off gasoline cargo from Japan (25.1 kbd, 16–22 Mar) — its first gasoline import of the crisis — before reverting to zero, suggesting CPC Corp’s domestic production remains adequate for now but was briefly stress-tested as crude feedstock shifted.
taiwan_naphtha: Taiwan’s naphtha imports collapsed 100% — from 175.9 kbd (2–8 Mar, sourced from Singapore and US) to zero in 23–28 Mar, after a brief pivot to UAE naphtha (127.5 kbd in 9–15 Mar, then 94.9 kbd in 16–22 Mar) that subsequently dried up.

The complete loss of naphtha feedstock threatens Formosa Plastics and CPC Linyuan crackers; Iraq’s FM compounded the supply loss by removing a secondary source (19.9 kbd in 9–15 Mar), and with South Korea’s naphtha export ban, regional availability is near-zero.
